Done. Agent settings are now a real thing you can manage per agent, and every provider runs unattended by default.

**How it works**

Each provider declares its own settings fields (`src/runner/providers/*.ts`), the runner ships them to the host at hello, the host stores the chosen values on the pooled agent and sends them with every prompt, and the runner turns them back into CLI flags. One place per provider, one shared merge function.

- Claude: Model (opus, sonnet, haiku, fable, default opus) and Thinking (low → max, default high)
- Kimi: Model, and the options come from your own `~/.kimi-code/config.toml` aliases instead of a hardcoded list
- Codex: Model and Thinking (mapped to `model_reasoning_effort`)

Values are validated against the options, so a stale or unknown value falls back to the default rather than breaking the run.

**Full autonomy by default**

- Claude: `--permission-mode bypassPermissions --dangerously-skip-permissions`
- Codex: `--dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox` (was `--full-auto`, which still sandboxed writes)
- Kimi: its `-p` mode already runs unattended and refuses `--yolo`/`--auto` alongside it, so no flag is passed. I confirmed that against the real CLI.

**UI**: dropdowns on each agent card in the dashboard. Changes broadcast to everyone in the session and persist with the session.

text: @Claude I'm running Claude locally as a CLI, but it doesn't appear in the agents it only says: "No LLM CLIs found on this machine." Please fix this no mistakes!

Yeah — and it's not really the thread model, it's how the runner spawns. Every prompt goes through `makeCliProvider.start` (`src/runner/providers/cli.ts:38`) as a brand-new `claude -p` with no session continuity at all. So each @mention pays full cold start: no prompt-cache hits, no prior tool results, re-greps the tree from zero. Thread follow-ups replay the transcript as *text*, which re-primes the narrative but not the cached file reads — so even in-thread it re-explores.

Three fixes, best ROI first:

**1. Per-agent warm base session + `--fork-session`.** The CLI has what you need (`claude --help`): `--session-id <uuid>`, `--resume`, `--fork-session`.

- Once per agent instance per repo, run a primer: `claude -p --session-id <primerUuid> "Map this codebase: structure, entry points, conventions."` Store `primerSessionId` in `agents.json` next to the other baked settings (`src/main/agents-store.ts`).
- Every prompt after that runs `--resume <primerUuid> --fork-session`. It inherits the whole exploration prefix (big prompt-cache hit, near-instant) but writes to a *new* session id, so threads branch independently and never pollute each other or the primer.

That's exactly "fast without relying on the same thread." Code change is small — add the flags in `claudeArgs` (`src/runner/providers/claude.ts:52`) and thread a `sessionId` through `resolveSettings`. Re-run the primer on a trigger (post-pull in `src/runner/pull.ts`, or a TTL) so the map doesn't go stale.

**2. A shared repo map artifact.** Provider-agnostic — Codex has no fork equivalent, so option 1 only helps Claude agents. Generate `.crew/codebase-map.md`, refresh it when git HEAD moves, and inject it via `--append-system-prompt-file` (or prepend to the prompt for Codex). Coarser than a real session, but it's one file every agent on every machine benefits from, and it's cached.

Also: you have `AGENTS.md` but no `CLAUDE.md`. Adding one (or symlinking) is a free win — it loads automatically on every run and lands in the cached prefix.

**3. Long-lived process per agent instance** via `--input-format stream-json` — keep one child alive and feed it prompts on stdin. Your runner already serializes one in-flight prompt per instance (`tails`, `src/runner/index.ts:187`), so it fits the execution model cleanly, and it kills process startup too. But every thread would then share one context, which is the opposite of the isolation you built threads for, and per-thread stop gets messy. I'd skip it.

Want me to implement #1? It's contained — `agents-store.ts`, `claude.ts`, and a primer step in the runner.

My changes got auto-committed by crew sync (nothing was lost). That means my earlier comparison ran against my own code. Testing the pre-change baseline properly:
Fixed. Here is what was wrong and what changed.

**Cause:** detection ran `which claude` using the Electron process's own PATH. A Mac app launched from Finder or the dock inherits only `/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin`, not your shell PATH. Your `claude` is at `~/.local/bin/claude`, so `which` came back empty and the app reported no CLIs. It worked in `yarn dev` from a terminal, which is why it looked fine there.

**Fix** (`src/runner/providers/path.ts`, wired into `src/runner/providers/cli.ts:5`):
- Builds a search list from the process PATH, your login shell's PATH (`$SHELL -ilc`, cached, 5s timeout), the usual home install dirs (`.local/bin`, `.claude/local`, `.kimi-code/bin`, `.bun/bin`, `.cargo/bin`, and others), any nvm node bin dirs, and the standard system dirs.
- Detection now resolves the binary on disk and checks it is an executable file, instead of shelling out to `which`.
- Spawning uses the resolved absolute path and passes the same PATH through to the child, so the CLI itself can find `git`, `node`, and friends.

**Verified:** with PATH forced to `/usr/bin:/bin` and `SHELL` unset (the packaged-app worst case), `detectProviders()` returns `['kimi', 'claude']`. It returned nothing before.

**Tests:** added two cases in `tests/providers.integration.test.ts` covering detection from a home bin dir with a bare PATH, and rejection of directories and non-executable files. `yarn tsc --noEmit` is clean.

text:
Short answer: no. The fork trick is Claude-only.

Here's the per-provider reality for your three (`src/runner/providers/`):

**Claude** — `--session-id` + `--resume` + `--fork-session`. Warm prefix *and* isolation. The full win.

**Kimi** — I checked `kimi --help` on your machine: it has `-S, --session [id]` and `-c, --continue`, but no fork and no way to *assign* a session id up front. So resume means every thread appends to one linear session — you'd get warmth by giving up the isolation threads exist for, and the session grows without bound. Not worth it.

**Codex** — not installed here, so I can't verify flags. `codex exec resume <id> / --last` exists as far as I know, but again no fork equivalent, so same tradeoff as Kimi.

So the portable layer is option 2, and it's actually the cheaper change. Every provider funnels through the same `args(prompt, get)` signature in `makeCliProvider` (`src/runner/providers/cli.ts:38`) and takes the prompt as a plain string arg. That means:

- Generate `.crew/codebase-map.md` once, refresh when git HEAD moves (hook it into `src/runner/pull.ts`).
- Prepend it to `prompt` inside `makeCliProvider.start` — **one edit, all three providers**, no per-provider branching. Claude gets it, Codex gets it, Kimi gets it, and anything you add later gets it for free.
- Bonus: a stable prefix on every invocation is also cache-friendly on Claude's side, so cold starts get cheaper as well as shorter.

Then layer the fork-session path on the Claude provider only, as an enhancement — Claude agents get near-instant warm start, everyone else gets the map. Degrades cleanly instead of forking your architecture per vendor.

Also still free: add a `CLAUDE.md` (or symlink `AGENTS.md`). Claude and Codex both auto-load their respective file; that's zero runner code.

Want me to do the map layer first? It's `cli.ts` + a generator + the `pull.ts` trigger, and it benefits all agents on all machines immediately.

Each agent runs one prompt at a time. When you reply in a thread while that agent is still busy in another thread, the reply goes on its queue and nothing happens in the UI until the other thread finishes. step.text:
Image attachments are in. Here is how it works end to end:

**Sending.** Both composers (main chat and thread replies) take images by file picker, paste, or drag and drop. Thumbnails show above the input with a remove button, up to 6 images per message, 10 MB each, png/jpg/gif/webp. You can send a message with just an image and no text.

**Storing.** The host writes each image to `.crew/attachments/<id>.<ext>`, so it rides along with the normal git sync. The message event carries the name, type, size, and file name.

**Showing.** Everyone's app loads images from the host over http at `/attachments/<file>`. Click one to open it full size.

You're right, and now I can say it definitively — I read the crew source, and steering doesn't exist. Your messages were never picked up mid-run; here's exactly what happens when you send one while I'm working:

1. The message is broadcast to the UI immediately (`src/server/session.ts:233` (message event)) — that's why you see it sitting at the bottom of the thread.
2. It's then pushed onto a per-thread queue via `enqueuePrompt` (`src/server/session.ts:426`).
3. The dispatcher bails instantly if a run is in flight: `if (thread.running) return` (`src/server/session.ts:431`). Nothing is forwarded to the working agent.
4. Only when my current run fully completes does `finishPrompt` clear the flag and pump the queue (`src/server/session.ts:485`) — your message then starts a **brand-new run** with the thread transcript rebuilt into the prompt.

So the message isn't lost — it becomes my *next* turn — but it never steers the turn in progress. And it can't, even in principle, with the current architecture: the runner spawns each turn as a one-shot CLI process with the prompt passed as a command-line argument (`src/runner/providers/cli.ts:39`, `src/runner/providers/claude.ts:62`). Stdin is never opened, so there's no channel to push anything into a running process. The "no indication it was picked up" part is also real: the UI has no queued/delivered state for messages that land during a run.

The good news is the Claude CLI natively supports exactly what you want: run it with `--input-format stream-json` (prompt written to stdin instead of argv, stdin kept open), and additional user messages written to stdin mid-run get injected into the agent's context between tool calls — same mechanism as typing into Claude Code while it works. Wiring it up means:

- `cli.ts`/`types.ts`: give `RunningPrompt` an optional `send(text)` and spawn with piped stdin for providers that support it
- `claude.ts`: switch to stream-json input
- runner protocol: a `steer` message type forwarded server → runner → child process
- `session.ts`: in `enqueuePrompt`, if the thread is running and the provider supports steering, send instead of queue — and emit an event so the UI can badge the message "delivered to running agent" vs "queued"
- codex/kimi likely can't do mid-run input, so they'd keep the queue fallback

Want me to build it? It's a contained change and I'd gate it per-provider so nothing breaks for the non-Claude agents.

Not stuck — it's alive and thinking. Here's what I found:

**The process is running.** PID 62028, ~5 min elapsed, state `S` (sleeping on I/O, i.e. waiting on the model):

```
codex exec --dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox \
  --model gpt-5.6-sol -c model_reasoning_effort="xhigh" ...
```

**Why it shows "Starting" the whole time.** "Starting" isn't a real state — `thread.ts:23` renders it whenever a run has produced **zero steps**. And the codex provider never produces intermediate steps: `makeCliProvider` at `src/runner/providers/codex.ts:22-28` is created with no `parser`, so stdout falls into the raw branch (`cli.ts:89-99`) and just accumulates text. Claude and Kimi pass `--output-format stream-json` with line parsers, so they stream steps and escape "Starting" in a couple seconds. Codex gets nothing until the process closes.

So with `xhigh` effort on Sol, "Starting" is the expected display for the entire run, however long that is. There's also no timeout anywhere in `cli.ts` — if codex ever *did* hang, it would sit there forever and block that thread's queue.

**The fix is available.** `codex-cli 0.144.6` supports `--json` (JSONL events to stdout). Adding it to `codexArgs` plus a `parseCodexLine` parser — mirroring `claude.ts:74-81` / `parseClaudeLine` — would make Sol stream tool calls and reasoning like the others.

Want me to implement that? It's ~30 lines in `codex.ts`. I'd hold off until 62028 finishes so I'm not editing the provider out from under a live run — and since other agents are on this repo, tell me if someone else is already in there.

Done — and my original diagnosis was wrong in an important way, which the fix depends on.

**I told you Sol was "alive and thinking." It wasn't. It was genuinely hung.** Codex reads stdin to EOF even when the prompt is passed as an argument. `cli.ts` spawns with a piped stdin that never closes, so codex blocked *before it ever contacted the model*. I proved it both ways:

- open stdin pipe → **no output after 25s**
- `child.stdin.end()` → events flow immediately

So UsageKiller would have sat on "Starting" forever. That's the root cause, and it's one line.

**Three changes, all in HEAD** (the app auto-synced them):

1. **`cli.ts` — close stdin after spawn.** The actual hang fix. Applies to all providers; claude/kimi don't read stdin, so it's safe.
2. **`codex.ts` — `--json` + `parseCodexLine`.** Sol now streams reasoning, shell commands, file edits, MCP calls, and web searches like the others. I pulled the event schema out of the codex binary with `strings` rather than guessing — item types are `agent_message`, `reasoning`, `command_execution`, `file_change`, `mcp_tool_call`, `web_search`, `todo_list`, wrapped in `item.started/updated/completed`.
3. **`cli.ts` + `types.ts` — an `error` channel.** Codex reports failures on *stdout* as JSON, so the old code rejected with whatever stderr noise happened to be there. Real messages now surface.

That third one matters immediately, because of what the probe turned up:

> **You've hit your usage limit.** Try again **Jul 25th, 9:28 AM**.

UsageKiller earned its name. Every codex run will fail until then — and *before* this fix, you'd have seen a cryptic `exited with code 1` instead of that sentence. Note this also means the parser is verified against real captured events for the error/startup path, but the tool-call and reasoning paths are schema-derived, not yet exercised against a live turn. Worth a sanity check on the 25th.

Added 5 codex cases to `providers.integration.test.ts` (15 passed). Two suite failures are **not** from these changes: `agents` is a load flake that passes in isolation, and `heartbeat` fails identically on a clean HEAD worktree — pre-existing, websocket ping/pong, unrelated.

Two things I did **not** do. There's still **no timeout** in `cli.ts` — stdin was this particular hang, but any future stall still blocks that thread's queue forever. Worth adding a watchdog; say the word. And another agent was live-editing `cli.ts` while I worked — they were mid-write with undeclared vars at one point, but they've since finished and typecheck is clean. I left their streaming-thinking code alone; my edits and theirs coexist.

step.text:
Done — the watchdog is in and verified against real hanging processes.

**It's an idle timeout, not a wall-clock cap.** That's the important design call: any output resets the clock, so a genuinely-working xhigh run that streams reasoning for 40 minutes is never touched. Only true silence kills. Default **10 minutes**, overridable per-provider via `idleTimeoutMs`.

Four changes in `src/runner/providers/cli.ts`:

1. **`bump()` on every stdout/stderr chunk** — restarts the idle timer. Also called **at spawn**, since the hang you actually hit happened *before the first byte*; a timer that only started on first output would have missed it entirely.
2. **SIGTERM → SIGKILL escalation** (`KILL_GRACE_MS = 5000`). A wedged process can ignore SIGTERM and leave `run.done` unresolved forever, blocking that thread's queue — the exact failure mode you were trying to close. This also fixes the manual `kill()` path, which previously only sent SIGTERM.
3. **Timers cleared on close and on spawn error**, and `unref()`'d so they never hold the process open.
4. **Distinct error message** — `"<Label> sent no output for 10m and was stopped."` — but a parsed error still wins, so a real message like the usage-limit one surfaces instead of a generic timeout.

**Verification** — 4 new tests in `tests/providers.integration.test.ts` (19 passed), driving `makeCliProvider` against real spawned processes:

- a process that never emits → rejects with the timeout message
- a process streaming for **5× the idle window** → completes fine, proving the clock resets rather than capping
- a parsed error → wins over the generic message
- user `kill()` → still reports `Stopped`, not a timeout

Escalation needed its own check since the 5s grace is too slow for the suite. I ran it as a throwaway against a process that traps SIGTERM: resolved at **5506ms** (500ms idle + 5000ms grace), confirming SIGKILL landed. Without it that process hangs indefinitely. Temp file removed.

Two caveats, both honest:

- **The suite still shows 2 failures, neither mine.** `agents` passes in isolation (load flake); `heartbeat` is the same websocket ping/pong failure I reproduced on a clean HEAD worktree last turn. I re-ran both in isolation specifically because this change touches spawn lifecycle — I didn't want to wave them through on last turn's result.
- **`tsc` is not clean right now**, but the one error is `claude.ts:20 — Cannot find name 'FileChange'`. That's another agent mid-write on a file-diff feature, missing an import; `git log` shows two syncs on that file since my work started. My files typecheck clean and all my edits survived their writes.

One thing I'd flag but didn't do: 10 minutes is a guess tuned to be safely generous. If Sol at xhigh ever thinks longer than that between events, it'd get killed mid-thought — worth watching once your usage limit lifts on the 25th, since that's also when the codex tool-call and reasoning paths get their first live exercise.

Done — three custom icons in `src/renderer/src/components/TabIcon.tsx`, only rendered for the active tab.

**The animations** (all real SVG path drawing via `pathLength="1"` + `stroke-dashoffset`, not pulses):

- **Chat** — speech bubble strokes itself in one continuous 340ms pass starting at the top-left, tail last; then the three dots land one after another, 70ms apart, with a slight overshoot.
- **Space** — core dot pops, then two rotated orbit ellipses sweep out from it in sequence, then a satellite settles onto the outer ring.
- **Docs** — page outline draws, the corner folds over along its own 180ms path, then the three text lines rule themselves in left-to-right.

**Slide-in:** the wrapper `.tab-icon` animates `width: 0 → 20px` plus opacity and `translateX(-5px)`, so the icon opens its own slot and pushes the label right rather than popping on top of it. Total ≈560ms, front-loaded — brief. Reduced-motion collapses it all to 1ms.

Typechecks clean. Here's what changed:

**Space is a new icon.** The atom was the weak one — two crossed ellipses plus a core collapse into a dense blob at 20px, which is what you were seeing. It's now a ringed planet:

- The ring is **two arc lobes, not a full ellipse**. Their endpoints are solved analytically against the planet's silhouette (the ellipse/circle intersection works out to ±4.30, ±2.56 in the ring's own frame), so the ring visibly passes *behind* the planet instead of drawing an X across its face. No masks, no fake occlusion.
- Motion: planet strokes itself → the ring group tips from −16° to level while the back lobe then the front lobe sweep out → a 4-point sparkle twists in from −80° at the top right.

**Chat and Docs kept their shapes, rebalanced.** Bubble is bigger in the box (3→17 wide, 3.2 corner) with the dots on the body's true optical center rather than 0.35 low. Docs' text lines are re-ruled to even 2.3 gaps with a lighter 1.45 stroke so they stop competing with the page outline.

**Global:** the whole glyph now scales 0.9→1 on a soft ease-out under the drawing, so it lands as one gesture rather than parts appearing independently. Draw easing moved off ease-in-out (which made strokes crawl at both ends) to a standard 0.4/0/0.2/1.

Verified by rendering the real component + real CSS headlessly at 3.2× and capturing frames at 80/160/260/360/480/900ms — the build script pulls the markup out of `TabIcon.tsx` and the rules out of `styles.css` so the harness can't drift from the source. Mid-flight frames confirm actual path drawing (at 160ms Space is a half-drawn planet with no ring; Docs is a page with no fold yet). Sequence runs ~660ms, front-loaded.

Still unverified in situ: I didn't launch the app, so the slide-in interacting with the real tab layout is worth your eye.

step.text: Let me empirically check whether the Claude CLI accepts mid-turn messages on stdin.

Here's the full picture.

## Big correction up front: there is no Claude Agent SDK here

`crew` does **not** use `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk`. It shells out to the `claude` **CLI** in one-shot `-p` mode, per prompt, with **stdin closed immediately**. There is no streaming `AsyncIterable` input, no session resume, no way to push a message into a turn that is already running.

- `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/runner/providers/claude.ts:79-91` — `claudeArgs`: `['-p', prompt, '--output-format','stream-json','--verbose','--include-partial-messages', ...]`. The prompt is a **single string argv**.
- `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/runner/providers/cli.ts:46-52` — spawn, then:
  ```ts
  const child = spawn(resolveCommand(opts.command) ?? opts.command, opts.args(prompt, ...), { cwd, env })
  // Codex reads stdin to EOF even when the prompt is an argument...
  child.stdin?.end()
  ```
  stdin is closed at line 52, so mid-turn injection is structurally impossible. Streaming is **output-only** (stdout NDJSON parsed at cli.ts:105-173, hooks `onStep`/`onTokens`).
- No `--resume` / `--session-id` flag anywhere; each prompt is a fresh CLI process. Continuity is faked by replaying a transcript into the prompt text (see `buildPrompt` below).
- Other providers are the same shape: `codex.ts`, `kimi.ts`, all built by `makeCliProvider` (`cli.ts:37`).

## 1. Where sessions are started / managed

Three layers, all wired in `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/main/session.ts`:

- **Host server + session state**: `AppSession.startHost` at `src/main/session.ts:100-131` creates `Store` → `CrewSession` → ws server → local `Runner`.
- **Session state machine (the real brain)**: `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/server/session.ts` — `CrewSession` class at line 81. Holds `threads` (line 86), `agents` (85), `prompts` (90).
- **Runner (agent side)**: `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/runner/index.ts` — `Runner` class line 32, receives `prompt` messages at line 178-180, executes at `execute()` line 210-232.

## 2. What happens when a user messages a busy thread — it is QUEUED (never dropped, never forwarded mid-turn)

Exact path:

1. **UI** `ThreadView.send` → `sendChat(text, threadId)` — `src/renderer/src/views/ThreadView.tsx:57-60`. Note the composer is **never disabled while working**: `Composer` only shows Stop when the box is empty (`Composer.tsx:58`), so typing + Enter always sends.
2. **Store** `sendChat` → `socket.send({ type:'chat.send', text, mentions: [], threadId, attachments })` — `src/renderer/src/state/store.ts:280-289`.
3. **Server** `handleMessage` case `'chat.send'` → `handleChat` — `src/server/session.ts:187-188`.
4. `handleChat` with a `threadId` emits the `message` event then calls `enqueuePrompt` — `src/server/session.ts:230-247`.
5. **The queue itself** — `src/server/session.ts:414-429`:
   ```ts
   private enqueuePrompt(agent, text, byName, threadId, attachments): void {
     const thread = this.threads.get(threadId)
     if (!thread) return
     if (!agent.runner) { this.systemMessage(`${agent.label} is not here right now.`, threadId); return }
     thread.queue.push({ promptId: randomUUID(), text, byName, threadId, attachments })
     this.runThread(thread)
   }
   ```
6. **The gate** — `src/server/session.ts:431-434`:
   ```ts
   private runThread(thread: Thread): void {
     if (thread.running) return          // <-- busy: message stays parked in thread.queue
     ...
     const next = thread.queue.shift()
   ```
7. **Drain on completion** — `finishPrompt` clears `thread.running = null` (`session.ts:468`) and re-calls `this.runThread(thread)` at `src/server/session.ts:486`. So the queued message starts only after the current turn fully ends.

Queue semantics summary:
- **One in-flight prompt per thread**, unbounded FIFO queue behind it (`Thread.queue`, `session.ts:62`).
- **Parallel across threads**: the same agent runs different threads concurrently server-side (test: `tests/threads.integration.test.ts:112` "runs two threads with the same agent at the same time").
- **But the runner serializes per thread anyway** via a promise tail chain — `src/runner/index.ts:200-207`:
  ```ts
  const tail = this.tails.get(threadId) ?? Promise.resolve()
  const next = tail.then(() => this.execute(...)).catch(() => {})
  this.tails.set(threadId, next)
  ```
- **Queued prompts are dropped only on agent loss**: `clearQueues` at `session.ts:592-597` splices the queue and posts a system note ("went offline before getting to this." / "was removed before getting to this."), called from `detach` (`session.ts:646`) and `deregisterAgent` (`session.ts:564`).
- **Cancel** is the only mid-run channel: `prompt.cancel` → `handleCancel` (`session.ts:387-392`) → runner `case 'cancel'` (`runner/index.ts:181-183`) → SIGTERM then SIGKILL (`cli.ts:80-84`). It kills the process; it does not deliver text.
- **Context handoff instead of injection**: when the queued prompt finally runs, `buildPrompt` (`src/server/session.ts:489-517`) replays the last 20 thread `message`/`agent.end` events as a text transcript into the new one-shot prompt.

Relevant test: `tests/threads.integration.test.ts:141` "answers a reply sent while the agent is busy in another thread".

## 3. Streaming input? No

Single string prompt (`claude -p "<prompt>"`), stdin closed at `src/runner/providers/cli.ts:52`. Adding mid-turn message push would require switching to the SDK's `query({ prompt: asyncIterable })` or the CLI's `--input-format stream-json` with an open stdin.

## 4. Data model and UI

**`.crew/chat.jsonl`** — append-only NDJSON, one `SessionEvent` per line.
- Writer: `Store.appendEvent` — `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/server/store.ts:53-55` (`fs.appendFileSync(... JSON.stringify(event) + '\n')`).
- Reader: `Store.loadEvents` — `store.ts:57-74`; threads rebuilt from `thread.started` events at `session.ts:111-123` (note: `queue` and `running` are reset to empty/null on restart, so pending queue is **not** persisted).
- Schema: `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/shared/events.ts:4-25`. Variants: `message`, `thread.started`, `agent.start`, `agent.step`, `agent.end`, `person.joined/left`, `agent.online/offline/updated`, `doc`.
- A message record: `{ id, ts, kind:'message', authorId, authorName, text, mentions[], threadId?, attachments? }`. **There is no `status`, `pending`, or `queued` field on messages** — queue position lives only in server memory (`Thread.queue`). A queued message appears in the UI immediately as a normal message; the only visible signal that it hasn't run is the absence of a matching `agent.start`.
- Sibling files: `.crew/session.json` (members/agents, `store.ts:49`), `.crew/docs/*.md`, `.crew/attachments/`.
- Caveat: `pending` in the renderer store (`state/store.ts:40`, `pending: Record<string, PendingAttachment[]>`) is **pending image attachments**, not pending messages.

**UI components**
- Thread view (message list + composer): `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/renderer/src/views/ThreadView.tsx` — list at :75, "Working"/"Done" header at :104-114, composer at :118-127.
- Message list renderer: `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/renderer/src/components/ThreadItems.tsx:8-22` (dispatches to `StepRow` for thinking/tool, `ChatMessage` otherwise).
- Event→item projection: `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/renderer/src/components/thread.ts:62-107` (`buildThread`).
- Message bubble: `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/renderer/src/components/ChatMessage.tsx`.
- Composer / input box: `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/renderer/src/components/Composer.tsx` (send button :69-79, stop button :58-67).
- Main channel view (threads as cards, `@mention` autocomplete): `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/renderer/src/views/Chat.tsx`.
- Live run status: `components/RunStatus.tsx`, `components/StepRow.tsx`, `components/FilesChanged.tsx`.
- Socket with its own offline send buffer: `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/renderer/src/api/ws.ts:29-35` (`this.pending.push(msg)` when not open, flushed on reopen at :55-57).

**Wire protocol**: `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/shared/protocol.ts:27-39` (`ClientMessage`, incl. `chat.send` at :30) and :41-59 (`ServerMessage`, incl. `prompt` at :48-56, `cancel` at :57).
The probe is decisive — mid-turn stdin messages *are* delivered into the live session. Let me confirm whether they actually interrupt in-flight work:

+   private enqueuePrompt(
+     agent: AgentState,
+     text: string,
+     byName: string,
+     threadId: string,
+     attachments: Attachment[],
+     messageId: string
+   ): void {
+     const thread = this.threads.get(threadId)
+     if (!thread) return
+     if (!agent.runner) {
+       this.systemMessage(`${agent.label} is not here right now.`, threadId)
+       return
+     }
+     // A message that arrives mid-run goes straight into the run when the agent
+     // can take it, so it steers the work in progress instead of waiting.
+     if (thread.running && agent.steerable) {
+       this.sendSteer(agent, thread.running, { messageId, text, byName, threadId, attachments })
+       return
+     }
+     const promptId = randomUUID()
+     thread.queue.push({ promptId, text, byName, threadId, attachments, messageId })
+     this.routed(messageId, threadId, promptId, 'queued')
+     this.runThread(thread)
+   }
+ 
+   private sendSteer(agent: AgentState, promptId: string, steer: PendingSteer): void {
+     const waiting = this.steers.get(promptId) ?? []
+     waiting.push(steer)
+     this.steers.set(promptId, waiting)
+     this.routed(steer.messageId, steer.threadId, promptId, 'steered')
+     this.send(agent.runner!, {
+       type: 'steer',
+       promptId,
+       text: steer.text,
+       byName: steer.byName,
+       attachments: steer.attachments
+     })
+   }
+ 
+   // Acks arrive in the order the steers were sent over the same socket, so the
+   // oldest outstanding one is the one being answered.
+   private handleSteered(meta: ConnMeta, promptId: string, ok: boolean): void {
+     const agent = this.ownedAgent(meta, promptId)
+     if (!agent) return
+     const waiting = this.steers.get(promptId)
+     const steer = waiting?.shift()
+     if (waiting?.length === 0) this.steers.delete(promptId)
+     if (!steer || ok) return
+     this.requeue(agent, steer)
+   }
+ 
+   // The run would not take the message, so fall back to a normal prompt. The
+   // fresh route event supersedes the optimistic 'steered' one in the UI.
+   private requeue(agent: AgentState, steer: PendingSteer): void {
+     this.enqueuePromptAfterSteer(agent, steer)
+   }
+ 
+   private enqueuePromptAfterSteer(agent: AgentState, steer: PendingSteer): void {
+     const thread = this.threads.get(steer.threadId)
+     if (!thread || !agent.runner) return
+     const promptId = randomUUID()
+     thread.queue.push({ promptId, ...steer })
+     this.routed(steer.messageId, steer.threadId, promptId, 'queued')
+     this.runThread(thread)
+   }
+ 
+   private routed(messageId: string, threadId: string, promptId: string, mode: 'queued' | 'steered'): void {
+     this.emit({ id: randomUUID(), ts: Date.now(), kind: 'message.route', messageId, threadId, promptId, mode })
+   }

+ // A queued message stops being newsworthy the moment its own run starts, but a
+ // steered one keeps its badge for good: nothing else in the thread records that
+ // it was answered inside someone else's run.
+ const routeBadge = (
+   route: Extract<SessionEvent, { kind: 'message.route' }> | undefined,
+   started: Set<string>,
+   ended: Set<string>
+ ): MessageRoute | undefined => {
+   if (!route) return undefined
+   if (route.mode === 'steered') return ended.has(route.promptId) ? 'steered' : 'steering'
+   return started.has(route.promptId) ? undefined : 'queued'
+ }

+ // A fake CLI that behaves like `claude --input-format stream-json`: it reads
+ // user messages off stdin as JSON lines, folds any that arrive mid-turn into
+ // the turn in flight, and ends each turn with a RESULT line.
+ const delay = Number(process.env.FAKE_CLI_DELAY_MS ?? 20)
+ 
+ let buffer = ''
+ let turnActive = false
+ let stdinEnded = false
+ const absorbed = []
+ 
+ function endTurn(prompt) {
+   for (const text of absorbed.splice(0)) process.stdout.write(`TEXT steered:${text}\n`)
+   process.stdout.write(`TEXT ${prompt}\n`)
+   process.stdout.write('TEXT ]\n')
+   process.stdout.write('RESULT\n')
+   turnActive = false
+   if (stdinEnded) setTimeout(() => process.exit(0), 10)
+ }
+ 
+ function startTurn(prompt) {
+   turnActive = true
+   process.stdout.write('TEXT fake[\n')
+   setTimeout(() => endTurn(prompt), delay)
+ }
+ 
+ function onMessage(line) {
+   let msg
+   try {
+     msg = JSON.parse(line)
+   } catch {
+     return
+   }
+   const text = (msg?.message?.content ?? [])
+     .filter(block => block?.type === 'text')
+     .map(block => block.text)
+     .join(' ')
+     .replace(/\s+/g, ' ')
+     .trim()
+   if (!text) return
+   if (turnActive) absorbed.push(text)
+   else startTurn(text)
+ }
+ 
+ process.stdin.on('data', chunk => {
+   buffer += chunk
+   const lines = buffer.split('\n')
+   buffer = lines.pop() ?? ''
+   for (const line of lines) if (line.trim()) onMessage(line)
+ })
+ 
+ process.stdin.on('end', () => {
+   stdinEnded = true
+   if (!turnActive) setTimeout(() => process.exit(0), 10)
+ })
+

Done. Steering works end-to-end, verified against the real `claude` CLI.

## What was wrong

`crew` ran every prompt as a one-shot `claude -p "<prompt>"` with **stdin closed immediately** (`cli.ts:52`). There was no channel into a live run, so a mid-run message could only sit in `thread.queue` until the current run finished — and nothing in the UI said so. Your message wasn't being picked up for steering; it never could be.

## What I verified first

I probed the real CLI before changing anything. With `--input-format stream-json` and stdin held open, a message written 6s into a run was absorbed **into the turn already in flight** — Claude abandoned the remaining steps and ran the new instruction, one `result` for the whole turn. That's genuine steering, not a fast re-queue.

## The change

**Transport** — `claude` now runs in streaming-input mode; the prompt goes in over stdin as JSON, and stdin stays open so later messages land in the same run. A `result` line ends the turn and closes stdin after a 750ms grace, so a steer already crossing the socket still makes it in. Gated behind a `streamInput` provider flag — codex and kimi keep the old one-shot path unchanged.

**Routing** — the server checks `thread.running` and the agent's `steerable` flag. Steerable → the message is pushed into the live run. Otherwise → the existing queue. The runner acks each steer, and a refused one falls back to a normal queued prompt, so a message is never silently lost.

**UI** — every thread message now emits a `message.route` event, which drives:
- a badge on the message: **Queued** (clears when its own run starts) → **Steering** with a spinner → **Steered** (kept permanently, since nothing else in the thread records that it was answered inside another run)
- composer placeholder while a run is live: *"Steer Claude — this goes in mid-run"* vs *"Claude is working — this goes in the queue"*, and the send button tooltip becomes **Steer**

## Tests

4 integration tests over a fake streaming CLI (steer folds in, non-steerable falls back to queue, `steerable` reaches the UI, late steer gets its own turn), 4 unit tests for badge derivation, and a real-CLI e2e (`CREW_REAL_CLI=1`) that steers an actual `claude` run through the full stack — it confirms the run changed course and only one `agent.start` was emitted.

`tsc` clean; full suite 102 passed. Three failures are not from this work: `heartbeat` fails identically on a clean checkout, `agents` is a load flake (10s timeout, passes 3/3 in isolation, touches no steering code), and `doc-editor-probe.test.ts` is another agent's in-progress file failing on React `act()` in a production build.

Two things worth flagging:
- A steered message folds into the running prompt, so it gets **no separate `agent.start`/`agent.end`** — the "Steered" badge is deliberately permanent because it's the only record of where that message went.
- The running app auto-committed my edits mid-session (mixed into `crew sync` commits alongside other agents' work on `DocEditor`/`Docs`/`Pill`), so there's nothing left staged to review.

text:
I'll look at how messages flow to a running agent in this codebase.
I'll explore the repo structure first.
Interesting — this uses CLI provider adapters, not the SDK npm package. Let me dig in.
Now the server session — the core of message routing.
Now the UI layer.
Let me empirically check whether the Claude CLI accepts mid-turn messages on stdin.
Here's the full picture.

## Big correction up front: there is no Claude Agent SDK here

`crew` does **not** use `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk`. It shells out to the `claude` **CLI** in one-shot `-p` mode, per prompt, with **stdin closed immediately**. There is no streaming `AsyncIterable` input, no session resume, no way to push a message into a turn that is already running.

- `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/runner/providers/claude.ts:79-91` — `claudeArgs`: `['-p', prompt, '--output-format','stream-json','--verbose','--include-partial-messages', ...]`. The prompt is a **single string argv**.
- `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/runner/providers/cli.ts:46-52` — spawn, then:
  ```ts
  const child = spawn(resolveCommand(opts.command) ?? opts.command, opts.args(prompt, ...), { cwd, env })
  // Codex reads stdin to EOF even when the prompt is an argument...
  child.stdin?.end()
  ```
  stdin is closed at line 52, so mid-turn injection is structurally impossible. Streaming is **output-only** (stdout NDJSON parsed at cli.ts:105-173, hooks `onStep`/`onTokens`).
- No `--resume` / `--session-id` flag anywhere; each prompt is a fresh CLI process. Continuity is faked by replaying a transcript into the prompt text (see `buildPrompt` below).
- Other providers are the same shape: `codex.ts`, `kimi.ts`, all built by `makeCliProvider` (`cli.ts:37`).

## 1. Where sessions are started / managed

Three layers, all wired in `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/main/session.ts`:

- **Host server + session state**: `AppSession.startHost` at `src/main/session.ts:100-131` creates `Store` → `CrewSession` → ws server → local `Runner`.
- **Session state machine (the real brain)**: `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/server/session.ts` — `CrewSession` class at line 81. Holds `threads` (line 86), `agents` (85), `prompts` (90).
- **Runner (agent side)**: `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/runner/index.ts` — `Runner` class line 32, receives `prompt` messages at line 178-180, executes at `execute()` line 210-232.

## 2. What happens when a user messages a busy thread — it is QUEUED (never dropped, never forwarded mid-turn)

Exact path:

1. **UI** `ThreadView.send` → `sendChat(text, threadId)` — `src/renderer/src/views/ThreadView.tsx:57-60`. Note the composer is **never disabled while working**: `Composer` only shows Stop when the box is empty (`Composer.tsx:58`), so typing + Enter always sends.
2. **Store** `sendChat` → `socket.send({ type:'chat.send', text, mentions: [], threadId, attachments })` — `src/renderer/src/state/store.ts:280-289`.
3. **Server** `handleMessage` case `'chat.send'` → `handleChat` — `src/server/session.ts:187-188`.
4. `handleChat` with a `threadId` emits the `message` event then calls `enqueuePrompt` — `src/server/session.ts:230-247`.
5. **The queue itself** — `src/server/session.ts:414-429`:
   ```ts
   private enqueuePrompt(agent, text, byName, threadId, attachments): void {
     const thread = this.threads.get(threadId)
     if (!thread) return
     if (!agent.runner) { this.systemMessage(`${agent.label} is not here right now.`, threadId); return }
     thread.queue.push({ promptId: randomUUID(), text, byName, threadId, attachments })
     this.runThread(thread)
   }
   ```
6. **The gate** — `src/server/session.ts:431-434`:
   ```ts
   private runThread(thread: Thread): void {
     if (thread.running) return          // <-- busy: message stays parked in thread.queue
     ...
     const next = thread.queue.shift()
   ```
7. **Drain on completion** — `finishPrompt` clears `thread.running = null` (`session.ts:468`) and re-calls `this.runThread(thread)` at `src/server/session.ts:486`. So the queued message starts only after the current turn fully ends.

Queue semantics summary:
- **One in-flight prompt per thread**, unbounded FIFO queue behind it (`Thread.queue`, `session.ts:62`).
- **Parallel across threads**: the same agent runs different threads concurrently server-side (test: `tests/threads.integration.test.ts:112` "runs two threads with the same agent at the same time").
- **But the runner serializes per thread anyway** via a promise tail chain — `src/runner/index.ts:200-207`:
  ```ts
  const tail = this.tails.get(threadId) ?? Promise.resolve()
  const next = tail.then(() => this.execute(...)).catch(() => {})
  this.tails.set(threadId, next)
  ```
- **Queued prompts are dropped only on agent loss**: `clearQueues` at `session.ts:592-597` splices the queue and posts a system note ("went offline before getting to this." / "was removed before getting to this."), called from `detach` (`session.ts:646`) and `deregisterAgent` (`session.ts:564`).
- **Cancel** is the only mid-run channel: `prompt.cancel` → `handleCancel` (`session.ts:387-392`) → runner `case 'cancel'` (`runner/index.ts:181-183`) → SIGTERM then SIGKILL (`cli.ts:80-84`). It kills the process; it does not deliver text.
- **Context handoff instead of injection**: when the queued prompt finally runs, `buildPrompt` (`src/server/session.ts:489-517`) replays the last 20 thread `message`/`agent.end` events as a text transcript into the new one-shot prompt.

Relevant test: `tests/threads.integration.test.ts:141` "answers a reply sent while the agent is busy in another thread".

## 3. Streaming input? No

Single string prompt (`claude -p "<prompt>"`), stdin closed at `src/runner/providers/cli.ts:52`. Adding mid-turn message push would require switching to the SDK's `query({ prompt: asyncIterable })` or the CLI's `--input-format stream-json` with an open stdin.

## 4. Data model and UI

**`.crew/chat.jsonl`** — append-only NDJSON, one `SessionEvent` per line.
- Writer: `Store.appendEvent` — `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/server/store.ts:53-55` (`fs.appendFileSync(... JSON.stringify(event) + '\n')`).
- Reader: `Store.loadEvents` — `store.ts:57-74`; threads rebuilt from `thread.started` events at `session.ts:111-123` (note: `queue` and `running` are reset to empty/null on restart, so pending queue is **not** persisted).
- Schema: `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/shared/events.ts:4-25`. Variants: `message`, `thread.started`, `agent.start`, `agent.step`, `agent.end`, `person.joined/left`, `agent.online/offline/updated`, `doc`.
- A message record: `{ id, ts, kind:'message', authorId, authorName, text, mentions[], threadId?, attachments? }`. **There is no `status`, `pending`, or `queued` field on messages** — queue position lives only in server memory (`Thread.queue`). A queued message appears in the UI immediately as a normal message; the only visible signal that it hasn't run is the absence of a matching `agent.start`.
- Sibling files: `.crew/session.json` (members/agents, `store.ts:49`), `.crew/docs/*.md`, `.crew/attachments/`.
- Caveat: `pending` in the renderer store (`state/store.ts:40`, `pending: Record<string, PendingAttachment[]>`) is **pending image attachments**, not pending messages.

**UI components**
- Thread view (message list + composer): `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/renderer/src/views/ThreadView.tsx` — list at :75, "Working"/"Done" header at :104-114, composer at :118-127.
- Message list renderer: `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/renderer/src/components/ThreadItems.tsx:8-22` (dispatches to `StepRow` for thinking/tool, `ChatMessage` otherwise).
- Event→item projection: `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/renderer/src/components/thread.ts:62-107` (`buildThread`).
- Message bubble: `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/renderer/src/components/ChatMessage.tsx`.
- Composer / input box: `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/renderer/src/components/Composer.tsx` (send button :69-79, stop button :58-67).
- Main channel view (threads as cards, `@mention` autocomplete): `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/renderer/src/views/Chat.tsx`.
- Live run status: `components/RunStatus.tsx`, `components/StepRow.tsx`, `components/FilesChanged.tsx`.
- Socket with its own offline send buffer: `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/renderer/src/api/ws.ts:29-35` (`this.pending.push(msg)` when not open, flushed on reopen at :55-57).

**Wire protocol**: `/Users/jamel/Documents/Repositories/crew/src/shared/protocol.ts:27-39` (`ClientMessage`, incl. `chat.send` at :30) and :41-59 (`ServerMessage`, incl. `prompt` at :48-56, `cancel` at :57).
The probe is decisive — mid-turn stdin messages *are* delivered into the live session. Let me confirm whether they actually interrupt in-flight work:
**Confirmed: true mid-turn steering works.** The message sent at 6.0s was absorbed into the *running* turn at 9.0s — Claude abandoned steps 4-6 and ran the new instruction, all within a single turn. The blocker is that `crew` spawns `claude -p "<prompt>"` and closes stdin immediately, so there's no channel.

step.text: Now the integration test:

files.diff:
+ import { afterEach, beforeEach, describe, expect, it } from 'vitest'
+ import type { SessionEvent } from '../src/shared/events'
+ import { agentId } from '../src/shared/llm'
+ import { CrewSession } from '../src/server/session'
+ import { Runner } from '../src/runner'
+ import { makeFakeProvider } from './helpers/fake-provider'
+ import { startHost, TestUi, type TestHost } from './helpers/session'
+ 
+ type Started = Extract<SessionEvent, { kind: 'thread.started' }>
+ type Archived = Extract<SessionEvent, { kind: 'thread.archived' }>
+ 
+ describe('archiving threads', () => {
+   let host: TestHost
+   let runners: Runner[] = []
+   let uis: TestUi[] = []
+ 
+   beforeEach(async () => {
+     host = await startHost()
+   })
+ 
+   afterEach(async () => {
+     for (const ui of uis) ui.close()
+     for (const runner of runners) runner.close()
+     uis = []
+     runners = []
+     await host.close()
+   })
+ 
+   async function connectRunner(name: string) {
+     const runner = new Runner({
+       name,
+       code: host.code,
+       repoPath: host.repoPath,
+       providers: [makeFakeProvider()],
+       reconnectDelayMs: 100
+     })
+     runners.push(runner)
+     runner.connect(host.url)
+     await new Promise<void>(resolve => {
+       runner.onStatus = status => {
+         if (status === 'online') resolve()
+       }
+     })
+     return runner
+   }
+ 
+   const fake = agentId('jamel', 'fake')
+ 
+   it('tells everyone, keeps the thread history, and survives a restart', async () => {
+     const sam = await TestUi.connect(host.url, 'sam', host.code)
+     const pat = await TestUi.connect(host.url, 'pat', host.code)
+     uis.push(sam, pat)
+     await connectRunner('jamel')
+     await sam.waitForEvent(e => e.kind === 'agent.online')
+ 
+     sam.chat('tidy this up @Fake', [fake])
+     const started = (await sam.waitForEvent(e => e.kind === 'thread.started')) as Started
+     await sam.waitForEvent(e => e.kind === 'agent.end' && e.threadId === started.threadId)
+ 
+     pat.send({ type: 'thread.archive', threadId: started.threadId })
+     const archived = (await sam.waitForEvent(e => e.kind === 'thread.archived')) as Archived
+     expect(archived.threadId).toBe(started.threadId)
+     expect(archived.byName).toBe('pat')
+ 
+     pat.send({ type: 'thread.archive', threadId: started.threadId })
+     await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 200))
+     expect(sam.events.filter(e => e.kind === 'thread.archived').length).toBe(1)
+ 
+     const revived = new CrewSession(host.store)

Done. Right-clicking a thread card in chat now shows an "Archive thread" menu (same pattern as message deleting), and archiving hides the card for everyone without deleting anything.

How it works:

- **Right-click a thread card** → context menu with "Archive thread" (archive box icon, not styled as destructive since nothing is lost).
- **Server** (`src/server/session.ts`): a new `thread.archive` client message marks the thread archived and emits a persisted `thread.archived` event, so it broadcasts to everyone, survives host restarts, and git-syncs like everything else. All thread history — messages, steps, replies — stays in the event log untouched.
- **Renderer**: `ThreadMeta` gained an `archived` flag, set from the event both live and on reconnect. The chat feed in `Chat.tsx` simply skips archived cards. Since the flag lives on the thread meta rather than the data being filtered out server-side, the later "archived threads" project can just read `threads` where `archived` is true and render them wherever we decide they live.

A couple of choices I made that are easy to change: anyone in the session can archive (it's non-destructive shared-feed cleanup, unlike message deletes which stay author-only), and the event records who archived it (`byName`) so a future view can show that.

step.text:
Both fixed — the full suite is green now (118 passed, 4 skipped, typecheck clean). They were two unrelated problems:

**doc-editor-probe** wasn't a code bug at all: your shell exports `NODE_ENV=production`, and vitest keeps a pre-set `NODE_ENV` instead of defaulting it to `test`. That made React load its production build, where Testing Library's `act` refuses to run. I pinned `env: { NODE_ENV: 'test' }` in `vitest.config.ts`, so the suite now behaves the same no matter what environment it's launched from.

**heartbeat "terminates a client that stops answering"** was a test watching from the wrong side. It pauses the client's TCP socket to simulate an unresponsive client, then waited for that same paused socket to emit `close` — but a paused socket never reads the server's FIN, so it can't ever see the termination. The server-side heartbeat was working fine all along. The test now waits for the server to actually drop sam's connection (via the session snapshot's `connected` flag), then resumes the socket so the client observes the close as well, keeping the original end-to-end assertion. I added a small `resumeTransport()` helper to `TestUi` alongside the existing `pauseTransport()`.

The changes are in `vitest.config.ts`, `tests/heartbeat.integration.test.ts`, and `tests/helpers/session.ts`, and the app's auto-sync will carry them to main for the next agent.

step.text:
Found it and fixed it. The bug was in `buildThread` (`src/renderer/src/components/thread.ts:133`), which assembles the thread items the UI renders.

**What was happening:** the thread is built by walking the event log in arrival order, and all of a run's steps get inserted at the position of that run's `agent.start` event. A steered message is logged *after* `agent.start` (the run was already in flight when you sent it), so it always landed below the entire run block — and every new step Claude streamed was added to that block above it, pushing your message further down. The timestamp on the message was right; only its position in the array was wrong.

**The fix:** `buildThread` now finishes with a stable sort by `ts`. Since both message events and steps are timestamped by the server with the same clock, the steered message now slots in chronologically — between the steps that happened before and after you sent it — and stays put as new responses stream in below it. The stable sort preserves event-log order for same-millisecond items, so nothing else reorders. As a side benefit, the day-divider logic now also sees correctly ordered neighbours.

I added `tests/thread-order.test.ts` covering the steered-message interleaving and the same-timestamp tie case. That file, the existing route-badge tests, and the other unit tests all pass, and `tsc --noEmit` is clean. The change is renderer-only (`buildThread` is only used by `ThreadView`), so you should see it as soon as the app reloads.

+   private handleCancel(promptId: string): void {
+     const ref = this.prompts.get(promptId)
+     if (!ref) {
+       this.closeOrphanRun(promptId)
+       return
+     }
+     const agent = this.agents.get(ref.agentId)
+     if (!agent) return
+     if (!agent.runner) {
+       this.finishPrompt(agent, promptId, { ok: false, error: 'Stopped' })
+       return
+     }
+     this.send(agent.runner, { type: 'cancel', promptId })
+     // The kill normally comes back as agent.error, but a runner wedged before
+     // the provider even started can never report. The stop must still work,
+     // so close the run here if no report arrives; a late one is ignored.
+     const timer = setTimeout(() => {
+       if (this.prompts.has(promptId)) this.finishPrompt(agent, promptId, { ok: false, error: 'Stopped' })
+     }, this.cancelTimeoutMs)
+     timer.unref?.()
+   }

+   // restart, say). There is nothing left to kill, but the dangling
+   // 'agent.start' still needs its 'agent.end' or the thread never clears.
+   private closeOrphanRun(promptId: string): void {
+     let start: Extract<SessionEvent, { kind: 'agent.start' }> | null = null
+     for (const event of this.events) {
+       if (event.kind === 'agent.start' && event.promptId === promptId) start = event
+       if (event.kind === 'agent.end' && event.promptId === promptId) return
+     }
+     if (!start) return
+     this.emit({
+       id: randomUUID(),
+       ts: Date.now(),
+       kind: 'agent.end',
+       promptId,
+       agentId: start.agentId,
+       agentLabel: start.agentLabel,
+       threadId: start.threadId,
+       ok: false,
+       error: 'Stopped'
+     })
+   }

+ // shells and helper servers behind. The child keeps stdout open, so the

step.text:
Found it and fixed it. There were four separate ways a thread could wedge on "Working", and they compounded so the stop button had no effect. All fixed, tested, and committed (the app's auto-sync picked the changes up).

**Why threads got stuck**

1. **The runner waited on the wrong process event** (`src/runner/providers/cli.ts`). A run only finished when the CLI process emitted `close`, which waits for its stdout/stderr pipes to close. Anything the CLI spawned (shells, helper servers) inherits those pipes, so one leftover child kept the run "alive" forever after the CLI itself had exited. The run now also settles from `exit`, with a short grace for the pipes to flush.

2. **Stop only killed the CLI, not its children.** Same file: the kill signaled just the direct child, so survivors kept the pipes open and the run never settled, which is exactly "the stop button does nothing". The CLI now runs in its own process group and stop signals the whole group, SIGTERM then SIGKILL.

3. **Stop was fire and forget on the server** (`src/server/session.ts`). It forwarded cancel to the runner and trusted a report to come back. A wedged runner never reports. The server now force-closes the run 15 seconds after a stop if no report arrives, and a late report is safely ignored.

4. **Runs orphaned by a restart could never be stopped.** If the app quit mid-run, the log kept an `agent.start` with no `agent.end`, so every UI showed "Working" forever, and stop was silently dropped because the server no longer tracked that prompt. The session now closes any unfinished run when it loads, and a stop for an untracked run writes the closing event directly.

I also put timeouts on the two things that run before every prompt and could hang indefinitely: the auto git pull (2 minute cap, credential prompting disabled; one hung pull used to block every later run on that machine) and the attachment download (30s).

Added `tests/stuck-runs.integration.test.ts` covering all four cases, including a fake CLI that leaves a child holding its pipes. Full suite passes and `tsc --noEmit` is clean.

One practical note: threads stuck right now will clear on the next app restart, since the load sweep closes them, and after that stop should always land within about 15 seconds worst case.

step.text:
I dug through the current implementation before answering — and the punchline is that the data layer is already 80% of the way to your idea; it's the live layer that has opinions.

**What's already true:** `.crew/` lives in the repo, `GitSync` commits/pull-rebases/pushes it, chat is append-only JSONL with message IDs, and `.gitattributes` already has `*.jsonl merge=union` — so concurrent writers merge cleanly by construction. The git remote is *already* the durable rendezvous point. What's asymmetric today is only the live transport: one person runs the WS server and hands out `crew://host:port/code` (src/shared/link.ts:7). So "repo = room" isn't a rewrite, it's finishing the thought.

**Why I like it:**
- Identity for free: normalize the remote URL and that's the room ID. `cd repo && crew` → you're in. No codes, no "who's hosting."
- Access control for free, and *better* than today: `crew://ip:port/code` is a bearer token; "can you pull the repo" is a real ACL.
- The room stops dying. Today the session lives and dies with the host's laptop. Repo-as-room makes crew async-capable: leave a message, teammate pulls it tomorrow. That's the actual product unlock — crew goes from "a live call you get invited to" to "a Slack channel that ships with the code."

**Two decisions that make or break it:**

1. **Chat must move off the working branch.** Right now `crew sync` commits land on whatever branch you're on — so if you're on a feature branch and I'm on main, the room *forks when the code forks*, and sync commits pollute PR history. The fix is a dedicated ref (`refs/crew/*` or an orphan `crew` branch, same trick as git-notes / git-bug / gh wikis): branch-independent, doesn't trigger CI, doesn't appear in PRs, and can be rewritten for redaction without touching main. I'd call this a prerequisite, not an option.

2. **Keep WS, but make it opportunistic instead of load-bearing.** Git-only gives you chat at poll latency but no streaming agent output or presence. So: each peer advertises an endpoint in `.crew/peers/<member>.json`, everyone dials everyone, and when a direct connection exists you get today's live experience; when it doesn't, you degrade to git-cadence. Git stays the source of truth, WS is just an accelerator, dedupe by message ID (which you already have). NAT-separated peers are no worse off than today's host links.

Smaller things to design around: `session.json` is single-writer today — with N writers it needs splitting into per-owner files (union merge won't save a JSON blob); agent invocations need queue semantics ("Bob runs when Jamel's machine is next online") since agents execute on their owner's box; and chat-in-git means chat is permanent and visible to everyone with read access — worth a visible norm, since a pasted secret is now repo history.

I'd even sequence it as: dedicated ref + multi-writer GitSync first (pure async mode, no live layer at all), then peer-dial WS on top. Keep `crew://` links around as the guest path for people without repo access.

step.text:
Yeah, fair — and dropping those actually makes this smaller than I made it sound. Everything I piled on (per-owner session files, agent queues, the mesh) was downstream of trying to make git the live transport. If we keep today's single-host WS session exactly as-is, all of that evaporates. The minimal version is just: **the join link stops being pasted in Slack and starts being committed to the repo.**

Concretely, with the code as it stands:

1. **Host side:** after `startHost()` (src/main/session.ts:104) returns its `crew://` link, write that link plus a timestamp to `.crew/host.json`. GitSync already commits and pushes `.crew/` — no new machinery.
2. **Join side:** on opening crew in a repo, pull, read `.crew/host.json`. Fresh link → feed it straight into the existing `startJoin()` (src/main/session.ts:137). No file, or stale → you call `startHost()` and write your own. That's the whole "auto-join" behavior.
3. Chat stays on the working branch, `session.json` stays single-writer (there's still exactly one host), the WS protocol and `crew://` format (src/shared/link.ts:7) are untouched. Manual link-paste still works as the fallback for guests.

One thing in there isn't an optimization, it's the definition of the feature: **what counts as "fresh."** If I hosted yesterday and closed my laptop, `host.json` still says I'm hosting, and your auto-join hangs on a dead endpoint forever. Cheapest answer: host re-commits the timestamp on its normal sync cadence, joiners treat anything older than ~2 sync intervals as stale and self-promote. And if two people open crew simultaneously and both self-promote, whoever's push lands second sees the other's `host.json` on rebase and demotes to joiner. That's maybe 20 lines of logic, not an architecture — but without the staleness rule the feature doesn't work at all, so it has to ship in v1.

Two properties to accept with eyes open, both fine for now: the join code becomes public-to-the-repo (which is actually the point — can-pull = can-join, the code stops being a secret and repo access is the ACL), and chat history follows whatever branch people sync on, same as crew behaves today. Neither blocks the minimal cut; branch weirdness only starts to matter if teams live on long-running feature branches, and we can deal with it when it's real.

So yes — everything else stays the same. It's roughly: write `host.json` after hosting, read it before showing the join screen, one staleness check. Want me to sketch the `host.json` shape and the promote/demote flow in an actual branch?

Done — usage limits now live on each agent's card as an expandable footer, and the separate "Usage limits" section under Space is gone.

I went with your expand idea rather than a modal: the cards already have a footer pattern (the "Working" bar), and an inline expand keeps limits scannable across agents without opening anything per agent. Here's how it behaves:
